revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:鉄鏃]]
'''Iron Arrowhead''' ({{ill|Tetsuzoku|ja|鉄鏃|1=Iron arrowheads|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tetsuzoku|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron arrowheads|qq=}}) is an iron-made {{ill|arrowhead|ja|鏃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arrowhead|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 矢|1=Arrow|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q45922}} that came into use in place of the earlier {{ill|stone arrowhead|ja|石鏃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone arrowhead|1=stone arrowhead|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587274}}.
== Overview == In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, iron arrowheads were not used as {{ill|hunting tools|ja|狩猟具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hunting tools|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 狩猟|1=Hunting|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q36963}} but as {{ill|Weapon|en|Weapon|qid=Q728|lt=weapons|1=Weapon}}, and they became widespread during the late {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} from the {{ill|2nd century|en|2nd century|qid=Q8103|lt=2nd century|1=2nd century}} to the {{ill|3rd century|en|3rd century|qid=Q8101|lt=3rd century|1=3rd century}}.
According to the account in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wae Ji Wajin Den|1=Wajinden}}, iron arrowheads were already common in northern {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1207048}} in the 3rd century during the time of {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai-koku|1=Yamatai}}, but they spread into the Kinai region only in the 4th century. In northern Kitakyushu, during the period preceding {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai-koku|1=Yamatai}}, conflicts over water rights and agricultural produce led to violent disputes, and at sites such as the {{ill|Yoshinogari site|en|Yoshinogari site|qid=Q1049645|lt=Yoshinogari Ruins|1=Yoshinogari site}}, remains have been found of bodies without heads and bodies pierced by both stone arrowheads and iron arrowheads. Moreover, the Yayoi period witnessed not only the use of iron for weapons but also the spread of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11649539}} in agricultural tools, implements, and fishing gear, a trend that was especially marked in the later period.
Because iron products were repeatedly melted down and reused, the quantity of iron artifacts recovered is not necessarily high, and it is difficult to establish a precise chronology. Nevertheless, even in the late Yayoi period, iron arrowheads appear to have been used in conjunction with stone and bronze arrowheads.
At a site in southern Okayama, dating to around the 2nd century BC, a dual-winged bronze arrowhead – which had been cast in China around the 4th–5th century BC – was excavated. As similar examples have not been found on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|qid=Q483134}}, it is considered likely that these items were brought directly from China.
== Classification and Manufacturing Process of Stemmed Types in the Yayoi Period == Iron arrowheads are broadly classified into two types: '''non-stemmed type''' ('''{{ill|non-stemmed type|ja|無茎式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/non-stemmed type|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''') and '''stemmed type''' ('''{{ill|stemmed type|ja|有茎式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stemmed type|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''').<ref>[[村上恭通]] 「シリーズ 日本史のなかの考古学『倭人と鉄の考古学』」 [[青木書店]] 1998th year ISBN 4-250-98070-7 p.73.</ref>
The stemmed type displays a variety of body shapes, and the stem ({{ill|Plant stem|en|Plant stem|qid=Q134267|lt=stem|1=Plant stem}}) that is attached to the arrow shaft is thicker than the body portion. These are divided into two subtypes: a "square, heavy type" and a "thin type" in which the thickness of the stem is nearly the same as that of the body, usually about 1 mm. The differences not only reflect variations in form, strength, and method of attachment, but also indicate differences in manufacturing techniques and quality. In the former, the stem gradually increases in thickness from the body and is carefully shaped by forging and hammering, whereas in the latter the thin, flat iron plate is shaped solely by cutting with a {{ill|Chisel|en|Chisel|qid=Q474188|lt=chisel|1=Chisel}}; thus, the former process is considerably more complex.<ref>[[村上恭通]] 同上 p.73.</ref>
== Shared Forms between Baekje and Wa == Around the 5th century, along with the adoption of a particular style of bow, long-necked arrowheads of the same style as those used on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Peninsula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Korean Peninsula|qid=Q483134}} began to be shared. This is thought to have been an effort to standardize bows and arrows in preparation for conflicts against {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, and it is also suggested by the fact that while western Japan used longbows, the ancient bows in northeastern Japan were relatively shorter, indicating that western Japan was influenced culturally (through military alliances and changes) by overseas expeditions.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://fieldnote.info/page-1424/page-1557/|title=岡安光彦 『原始和弓の起源』 2015th year |accessdate=}}</ref><ref>岡安光彦 2015th year </ref>
== Change in Weight == According to [[戸田智]]'s ''Analysis of the Functional Aspects of Iron Arrowheads and Bows in the Kofun Period'', the weight of iron arrowheads during the period from the mid-{{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生中期|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi period|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the end of the early Kofun period (2nd century to late 4th century) ranged from 4 to slightly over 6 grams, with some weighing as much as 7 grams. In contrast, from the late early period to the terminal period (late 4th century to the 8th century), they weighed around 15 grams, more than twice as heavy. [[Sahara Makoto]] in ''曾有戦争があった ―石鏃の変質―'' argued that with the spread of iron armor, iron arrowheads also became heavier.
== Examples of Use == The oldest iron artifacts in our country have been excavated from the {{ill|Saitoyama Ruins|ja|斎藤山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitoyama Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kumamoto Prefecture|en|Kumamoto Prefecture|qid=Q130308|lt=Kumamoto Prefecture|1=Kumamoto Prefecture}} and the {{ill|Nijōmachi Magarida Ruins|ja|二丈町曲り田遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijōmachi Magarida Ruins|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Additionally, 43 items have been unearthed from the {{ill|Yukinoyama Kofun|en|Yukinoyama Kofun|qid=Q11659952|lt=Yukino-yama Kofun|1=Yukinoyama Kofun}} in {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|qid=Q240891|lt=Higashiomi|1=Higashiōmi}} (formerly known as {{ill|Yōkaichi, Shiga|en|Yōkaichi, Shiga|qid=Q1042410|lt=Yōkaichi-shi|1=Yōkaichi, Shiga}}) in {{ill|Shiga Prefecture|en|Shiga Prefecture|qid=Q131358|lt=Shiga Prefecture|1=Shiga Prefecture}}. Iron arrowheads have been found not only in the context of defensive implements ({{ill|Yugi|ja|靫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箙|1=Yebira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17061580}}) but also at various other sites. It is believed that many of the larger specimens were not intended for actual combat but rather served a ceremonial, decorative function.<ref>林正憲「古墳時代前期前半の代表例 －雪野山古墳」/[[Nara Cultural Property Research Institute|奈良文化財研究所]] (2005) pp.145–146.</ref>
[[File:Kofun Era Iron Arrowheads (29949031282).jpg|thumb|200px|right|Iron arrowheads from the Kofun period]]
== Lethal Power in Bow and Arrow == According to an experiment conducted during a 2008 broadcast of the NHK/BS hi program "【{{ill|Einstein's Eye|ja|アインシュタインの眼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Einstein's Eye|1=Einstein's Eyes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11282514}}】: 'The Hidden Techniques and Power of Kyudo (Archery)'," it was demonstrated that, when shot from an extremely short distance, an iron-made {{ill|Frying pan|en|Frying pan|qid=Q127666|lt=frying pan|1=Frying pan}} of fixed thickness (a few millimeters) could be perforated. Naturally, the effective power of an iron arrowhead varies with the distance to a moving target, and at longer distances even protective garments made of cloth ({{ill|Horo (cloak)|en|Horo (cloak)|qid=Q1041083|lt=protective clothing|1=Horo (cloak)}}) can stop them, as the clothing retains a degree of elasticity due to wind pressure.
In the Kofun period, iron arrowheads did not always possess enough power to pierce through metal plates. According to a record in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'' in the annals of {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}}, on August 10 in the 12th year of his reign, many people were unable to shoot through an iron target presented by Korea, but only the archer Dateda Shukune (later known as Matoda Shukune) managed to penetrate it, prompting the Korean guests to rise and pay homage. As mentioned earlier regarding the "change in weight," in the 4th century the arrowheads were light, but with the spread of iron armor, they became heavier.
In the ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}}'', in the 7th year of the {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyo|1=Tenpyō}} era (735), it is recorded that "20 sets of arrows (20 powerful arrows that could also penetrate armor) were presented," indicating that improvements in arrow design progressed during the Ritsuryō period.
Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tongdian|en|Tongdian|qid=Q2615272|lt=Tsuutan|1=Tongdian}}'', it is recorded that even stone arrowheads, depending on the quality of the stone, were capable of penetrating iron. From the standpoint of durability, stone arrowheads could not necessarily be used repeatedly since their tips would break and require reworking, which would lighten them after repair. Consequently, while the initial impact force of both stone and iron arrowheads might be comparable if their weights were equal, with repeated use the power of stone arrowheads would diminish. In contrast, iron arrowheads can be recycled by remelting and recasting.
== References == <references responsive=""/> == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|author=[[独立行政法人文化財研究所]]・[[奈良文化財研究所]]監修|title=日本の考古学 －ドイツで開催された「曙光の時代」展|publisher={{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|qid=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan|1=Shogakukan}}|date=2005|isbn=4-09-681821-6}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Arrowhead|ja|鏃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arrowhead|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 矢|1=Arrow|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q45922}}
